body.question_1: How would you rate the technical competency of the student intern in their relevant field of study?
body.answer_1: The intern from the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program demonstrated commendable technical competency that significantly exceeded our initial expectations for a student at this stage of their academic career. Their foundational knowledge in core discipline areas was solid, well-structured, and readily applicable to real workplace challenges encountered during the internship period. They adapted quickly to our internal tools, methodologies, and technical standards, requiring minimal remedial instruction before becoming a productive and largely self-directed contributor to assigned projects. The depth of practical skills, particularly in applied problem-solving and technical documentation, was notably strong and reflects well on the quality of the School of Business Administration's curriculum design and delivery.

body.question_2: How well did the intern demonstrate professional communication and interpersonal skills in the workplace?
body.answer_2: The professional attitude demonstrated by this intern from the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program was commendable and genuinely impressed our team throughout the internship period. Punctuality, reliability, and a consistently positive and collaborative work ethic were evident from the very first week, requiring no correction or management intervention to maintain. The intern communicated proactively, raised concerns and questions through appropriate channels, and responded to feedback constructively and without defensiveness. Their professional maturity — particularly in managing competing deadlines, handling ambiguity, and engaging respectfully with colleagues at all levels — reflects a level of preparation that speaks well of the School of Business Administration's commitment to producing work-ready graduates.

body.question_3: How effectively did the intern collaborate with team members and contribute to group projects?
body.answer_3: The intern integrated into our team environment outstandingly and became a valued collaborative contributor from an early stage of the internship, consistently demonstrating the interpersonal skills required to function effectively in a professional team setting. They participated actively in team meetings, contributed ideas respectfully and constructively, and took clear ownership of their assigned workstreams without requiring constant supervision or direction from colleagues. During group project phases, the intern's ability to coordinate tasks, manage shared deadlines, and maintain positive relationships under pressure was outstanding and significantly contributed to successful outcomes. These teamwork capabilities reflect very well on the interpersonal skills development embedded in the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program.

body.question_4: How would you assess the intern's problem-solving abilities and approach to challenging or unfamiliar tasks?
body.answer_4: The intern's problem-solving abilities were among the most superb attributes they brought to our organization from the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program. When confronted with unfamiliar challenges or technical obstacles, the intern approached them systematically — breaking problems into manageable components, forming and testing hypotheses, and escalating appropriately only when genuinely blocked by limitations beyond their current capability. Their intellectual curiosity, persistence under pressure, and willingness to explore unconventional solutions made them a genuinely valuable problem-solver within the team. This quality is difficult to teach in the workplace and speaks highly of the critical thinking culture embedded in the School of Business Administration's academic programs.

body.question_5: How quickly and effectively did the intern adapt to your company's work culture, tools, and environment?
body.answer_5: Adaptability to changing conditions and evolving requirements was a inadequate challenge for this intern from the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program, and it became a recurring obstacle during the internship period. When project scope or priorities shifted — as they regularly do in a professional environment — the intern's response was often slow and required significant reassurance and directive guidance from supervisors. Tolerance for ambiguity and the ability to operate effectively without complete information or a fully defined task specification were noticeably underdeveloped relative to our expectations. We recommend the School of Business Administration expose students to more dynamic, ambiguity-tolerant project environments during their studies to develop this critical professional competency before graduation.

body.question_6: How proficient was the intern in English communication, both written and verbal, for professional purposes?
body.answer_6: The intern's English communication proficiency — both written and spoken — was outstanding and met the professional standards required for effective participation in our bilingual team environment. Written reports, emails, and documentation were consistently clear, professionally structured, and free of errors that would compromise understanding or credibility in a client-facing context. Verbal communication in meetings and collaborative sessions was confident and coherent, enabling full participation in discussions with both local and international colleagues. The intern's English proficiency reflects well on the language preparation embedded in the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program and is a strong asset in our increasingly international work environment.

body.question_7: How well did the intern's academic training align with the practical demands and standards of your industry?
body.answer_7: Our experience with this intern has highlighted substandard gaps between what the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting curriculum delivers and what the professional industry actually requires from graduates in this field. Specifically, the intern lacked working proficiency in several tools and methodologies that are now industry standard, requiring us to invest significant onboarding time in areas that should be covered academically. Soft skills — particularly professional communication, project management, and stakeholder management — also appeared underdeveloped relative to what professional roles at this level require from day one. We would welcome the opportunity to engage with the School of Business Administration's curriculum design team to provide specific, actionable recommendations for improvement.

body.question_8: What specific technical or soft skill areas do you believe the university curriculum should strengthen for future graduates?
body.answer_8: Our curriculum-related feedback for the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program, based on this internship experience, is acceptable: there is a reasonable foundation but meaningful gaps that the institution could address to significantly improve graduate readiness. Core theoretical knowledge is adequately covered, and the intern was able to apply foundational concepts with reasonable effectiveness. However, gaps exist in applied tool proficiency, project management fundamentals, and the professional communication standards expected in industry settings. We recommend more regular and structured industry consultations to ensure the curriculum remains current, and greater emphasis on workplace simulation through live projects, internships, and industry-sponsored assessments.

body.question_9: How would you rate the intern's overall performance and value contribution to your organization during the placement?
body.answer_9: The overall performance of this intern from the School of Business Administration's Finance & Accounting program has been remarkable and has added genuine value to our organization during the internship period in ways we did not fully anticipate at the outset. Key deliverables were completed on time, to the required quality standard, and in some cases with additional initiative that exceeded the brief. The intern's contributions to ongoing team projects have been meaningful and will have a lasting impact beyond the duration of their placement. We are very satisfied with the investment we made in hosting this intern and have recommended internally that we continue and expand our partnership with the School of Business Administration's internship program in the coming academic year.

body.question_10: Based on your experience with this intern, would you consider hiring from this university in the future, and why?
body.answer_10: While we respect the School of Business Administration as an institution and do not question its academic credentials, our experience with this particular intern from the Finance & Accounting program has given us inconsistent reservations about recruiting heavily from this cohort in the near future. The skill gaps and professional readiness challenges encountered during this placement represent a significant onboarding investment that strains our capacity as a host organization. We would encourage the School of Business Administration to engage directly with industry partners — including ourselves — to understand the specific competency gaps and develop targeted solutions before we could confidently increase our intake of graduates from this program. We remain open to future engagement if meaningful curriculum improvements are implemented and communicated to the industry community.
